literal: dispassion, freedom from coloring; abstract noun (vṛddhi-derivative with -ya) from vi-rāga 'without passion' (vi- 'away from' + rāga 'coloring, passion', from √rañj 'to be dyed/attached'), 'the state of being uncolored' — the absence of the mind's being dyed by objects
invariant_definition: *Vairāgya* is dispassion — the state of being 'uncolored' (*vi-rāga*) by objects, the settled absence of craving for what is seen or heard of. It is the positive counterpart of the cessation of *rāga*: not aversion to objects but freedom from their pull. Across the disciplines *vairāgya* is the indispensable companion of practice (*abhyāsa*): in Pātañjala Yoga the *vṛttis* are stilled 'by practice and dispassion' (*abhyāsa-vairāgyābhyām*, YS 1.12), and *vairāgya* is defined as the mastery (*vaśīkāra-saṃjñā*) of one who has ceased to thirst for objects experienced or reported (YS 1.15), with a higher (*para*) dispassion that follows the vision of *puruṣa* (YS 1.16). In Advaita *vairāgya* is one of the four qualifications (*sādhana-catuṣṭaya*) for inquiry — dispassion toward enjoyments here and hereafter — without which *śravaṇa* and the rest cannot take hold. In the *sthitaprajña* discourse the same condition appears as *vigata-spṛha* ('with craving gone', 2.56) and is the force behind 2.59's *rasa-varjam*: the man of knowledge has lost not merely contact with objects but the very relish (*rasa*) for them. *Vairāgya* is thus distinguished from forced abstention — the senses of the sick or the faster turn from objects while the relish remains; true dispassion is the disappearance of the relish itself, which, in the Vedāntic reading, only knowledge of the supreme can effect.

school_framing.shared_core: *Vairāgya* names **dispassion — the settled absence of craving for objects, the mind no longer 'colored' by them**. The disciplines converge in treating it as the necessary counterpart of practice and as distinct from mere abstention; they differ on whether dispassion is itself sufficient, a prerequisite to be supplemented by knowledge, or the fruit of realization.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Largely **shared** as a discipline-term. The framings differ in placement: Yoga makes *vairāgya* one of the two means of *nirodha* and grades it into ordinary and higher (post-puruṣa-vision) dispassion; Advaita makes it a qualification (*sādhana*) for inquiry; the Gītā reading treats the deepest dispassion (*rasa-varjam*) as itself a consequence of the vision of the supreme, so that here dispassion is as much fruit as means.

per_school.definition: *Vairāgya* is, with *abhyāsa* (practice), one of the two means of stilling the *citta-vṛttis* (YS 1.12). It is defined as the *vaśīkāra-saṃjñā* — the consciousness of mastery — of one who has ceased to thirst for objects experienced or heard of (YS 1.15); the higher *vairāgya* (*para-vairāgya*) is the thirstlessness toward even the *guṇas* that arises from the vision of *puruṣa* (YS 1.16). Dispassion is thus both an ordinary discipline and, at its summit, the effect of discrimination.

*Vairāgya* is the second of the four qualifications (*sādhana-catuṣṭaya*) for the inquiry into Brahman — dispassion toward the enjoyment of fruits here and in higher worlds — without which the means of knowledge (*śravaṇa*, *manana*, *nididhyāsana*) cannot bear fruit. In the *sthitaprajña* discourse Śaṅkara reads the deepest dispassion (the *rasa-varjam* of 2.59) not as forced abstention but as the disappearance of the very relish for objects, which only *samyag-darśana* — knowledge of the supreme — finally effects.
primary_loci: Gītā-Bhāṣya (Śaṅkara) on 2.59 (rasa-varjam; rasa as rāga, removed by samyag-darśana)

textual_history.summary: *Vairāgya* is a post-Vedic technical term built directly on *rāga*: it is the *vṛddhi*-abstract of *vi-rāga* ('de-coloured, without passion'), and since *rāga* itself is not a Vedic category neither is its negation — there is no Vedic *vairāgya*. The word belongs to the classical discipline-vocabulary. Pātañjala Yoga gives it its foundational definition: with *abhyāsa* (practice) it is one of the two means of stilling the mind (YS 1.12), defined as the mastery of one who has ceased to thirst for objects seen or heard of (1.15), with a higher *para-vairāgya* following the vision of *puruṣa* (1.16). The *Bhagavad-Gītā* names *vairāgya* with *abhyāsa* as the means of restraining the restless mind (6.35), and Advaita makes it the second of the four qualifications (*sādhana-catuṣṭaya*) for inquiry.

stages.rendering: By practice and dispassion (abhyāsa-vairāgya) there is the stilling of that [mind]. … Dispassion is the consciousness of mastery (vaśīkāra) of one who has become thirstless toward objects seen or heard-of.
stages.note: The earliest technical fixing in our corpus, and the source of the standard *abhyāsa-vairāgya* pairing. Note the definition turns on *vitṛṣṇa*, 'thirstless' — *vairāgya* is the absence of *tṛṣṇā* toward both experienced (*dṛṣṭa*) and merely reported, scriptural (*ānuśravika*) objects. The higher *para-vairāgya* (1.16) is thirstlessness even toward the *guṇas*, arising from *puruṣa-khyāti*.

stages.sanskrit: asaṃśayaṃ mahābāho mano durṇigrahaṃ calam | abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate
stages.rendering: Without doubt, mighty-armed one, the mind is hard to restrain and restless; but by practice and by dispassion (abhyāsa, vairāgya), Kaunteya, it is held in check.
stages.note: Kṛṣṇa's answer to Arjuna on the mind's restlessness echoes the Yoga formula exactly — *abhyāsa* and *vairāgya* as the twin means of *nigraha*. The Gītā's deepest dispassion is the *rasa-varjam* of 2.59, where Śaṅkara reads even the residual relish (rasa) as removed only by the vision of the Supreme — dispassion as fruit of knowledge, not mere abstention.

translator_note: 'Dispassion' or 'detachment' is accurate; 'renunciation' is not — that is *saṃnyāsa* (an outward giving-up), whereas *vairāgya* is the inward absence of craving and may be present with or without formal renunciation. Keep the literal image alive where it helps: *vi-rāga* is the mind *not colored* by objects, the negative of *rāga* (the coloring of attachment). The crucial distinction the texts draw is between *vairāgya* and mere abstention: the senses can be kept from objects while the relish (*rasa*) persists, which is not yet dispassion. True *vairāgya* is the disappearance of the relish itself.
